title: Area & perimeter on the coordinate plane
description: Find the area or perimeter of shapes like triangles, rectangles, parallelograms, and hexagons on the coordinate plane.
topic_path: Problem solving with distance on the coordinate plane
Area & perimeter on the coordinate plane

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the area, in square units, of rectangle $ABCD$ plotted below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{base} \cdot \text{height}$

Before we can apply the formula to find the area of rectangle $ABCD$, we must find the base and height of the rectangle using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

* The base is the length $BC$.
* The height is the length $AB$.

###Step 1: Find the length $BC$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
BC^2&= {\redE{1}^2 +\redE{4}^2 }  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
BC&= \sqrt{\redE{1}^2 +\redE{4}^2 }  &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\tealE{\sqrt{17}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Find the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
AB^2&= \redE{2}^2 +\redE{8}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{2}^2 +\redE{8}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\sqrt{68} \\\\
&=\sqrt{4 \cdot 17} \\\\
&=\goldE{2\sqrt{17}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Find the area of the rectangle

Using the base and height of rectangle $ABCD$, we can find the area:

$\begin{align} \text{Area of a rectangle} &= \text{base} \cdot \text{height}\\\\
&= BC\cdot AB \\\\
&= (\tealE{\sqrt{17}})\cdot(\goldE{2\sqrt{17}}) \\\\
&= 2\cdot\sqrt{17}\cdot\sqrt{17} \\\\
&= 2\cdot17 \\\\
&= 34
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The area of rectangle $ABCD$ is $34$ units$^2$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Rectangle A B C D has point A at negative four, five, point B at negative two, negative three, point C at two, negative two, and point D at zero, six.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the area, in square units, of $\triangle ABC$ plotted below.**


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

$\text{Area of a triangle} = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}$

Before we can apply the formula to find the area of $\triangle ABC$, we must find the base and height of the triangle using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

* The base is the length $AC$.
* The height is the length $AB$.

###Step 1: Find the length $AC$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
AC^2&= {\redE{6}^2 +\redE{15}^2 }  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AC&= \sqrt{\redE{6}^2 +\redE{15}^2 }  &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\sqrt{261} \\\\
&=\sqrt{9\cdot29} \\\\
&=\tealE{3\sqrt{29}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Find the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
AB^2&= \redE{5}^2 +\redE{2}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{5}^2 +\redE{2}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\goldE{\sqrt{29}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Find the area of the triangle

Using the base and height of $\triangle ABC$, we can find the area:

$\begin{align} \text{Area of a triangle} &= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot AC\cdot AB \\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot(\tealE{3\sqrt{29}})\cdot(\goldE{\sqrt{29}}) \\\\
&= \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\sqrt{29}\cdot\sqrt{29} \\\\
&= \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot29 \\\\
&= \dfrac{87}{2}
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The area of $\triangle ABC$ is $43.5$ units$^2$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Triangle A B C has point A at one, eight, point B at six, six, and point C at negative five, negative seven.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the area, in square units, of $\triangle ABC$ plotted below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

$\text{Area of a triangle} = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}$

Before we can apply the formula to find the area of $\triangle ABC$, we must find the base and height of the triangle using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

* The base is the length $AB$.
* The height is the length $CD$ (the base and the height always meet at a right angle).

###Step 1: Find the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
AB^2&= {\redE{2}^2 +\redE{6}^2 }  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{2}^2 +\redE{6}^2 }  &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\sqrt{40} \\\\
&=\sqrt{4\cdot10} \\\\
&=\tealE{2\sqrt{10}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Find the length $CD$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
CD^2&= \redE{3}^2 +\redE{1}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
CD&= \sqrt{\redE{3}^2 +\redE{1}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\goldE{\sqrt{10}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Find the area of the triangle

Using the base and height of $\triangle ABC$, we can find the area:

$\begin{align} \text{Area of a triangle} &= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot AB\cdot CD \\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot(\tealE{2\sqrt{10}})\cdot(\goldE{\sqrt{10}}) \\\\
&= \sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{10} \\\\
&= 10
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The area of $\triangle ABC$ is $10$ units$^2$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Triangle A B C has point A at negative four, negative six, point B at two, negative eight, and point C at negative six, negative two. There is a slanted dashed line connecting from point C to a point D at negative seven, negative five and a dashed line connects from point A to point D. The dashed lines make triangle A B C a right triangle at B C D.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the area, in square units, of rectangle $ABCD$ plotted below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

$\text{Area of a rectangle} = \text{base} \cdot \text{height}$

Before we can apply the formula to find the area of rectangle $ABCD$, we must find the base and height of the rectangle using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

* The base is the length $AB$.
* The height is the length $BC$.

###Step 1: Find the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
AB^2&= {\redE{12}^2 +\redE{4}^2 }  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{12}^2 +\redE{4}^2 }  &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\sqrt{160} \\\\
&=\sqrt{16\cdot10} \\\\
&=\tealE{4\sqrt{10}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Find the length $BC$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
BC^2&= \redE{2}^2 +\redE{6}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
BC&= \sqrt{\redE{2}^2 +\redE{6}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\sqrt{40} \\\\
&=\sqrt{4 \cdot 10} \\\\
&=\goldE{2\sqrt{10}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Find the area of the rectangle

Using the base and height of rectangle $ABCD$, we can find the area:

$\begin{align} \text{Area of a rectangle} &= \text{base} \cdot \text{height}\\\\
&= AB \cdot BC \\\\
&= (\tealE{4\sqrt{10}})\cdot(\goldE{2\sqrt{10}}) \\\\
&= 8\cdot\sqrt{10}\cdot\sqrt{10} \\\\
&= 8\cdot10 \\\\
&= 80
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The area of rectangle $ABCD$ is $80$ units$^2$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Rectangle A B C D has point A at negative six, negative six, point B at six, negative two, point C at four, four and point D at negative eight, zero.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the area, in square units, of $\triangle ABC$ plotted below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

$\text{Area of a triangle} = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}$

Before we can apply the formula to find the area of $\triangle ABC$, we must find the base and height of the triangle using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

* The base is the length $AB$.
* The height is the length $CD$ (the base and the height always meet at a right angle).

###Step 1: Find the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
AB^2&= {\redE{3}^2 +\redE{12}^2 }  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{3}^2 +\redE{12}^2 }  &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\sqrt{153} \\\\
&=\sqrt{9\cdot17} \\\\
&=\tealE{3\sqrt{17}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Find the length $CD$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
CD^2&= \redE{2}^2 +\redE{8}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
CD&= \sqrt{\redE{2}^2 +\redE{8}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\sqrt{68} \\\\
&=\sqrt{4\cdot17} \\\\
&=\goldE{2\sqrt{17}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Find the area of the triangle

Using the base and height of $\triangle ABC$, we can find the area:

$\begin{align} \text{Area of a triangle} &= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot AB\cdot CD \\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot(\tealE{3\sqrt{17}})\cdot(\goldE{2\sqrt{17}}) \\\\
&= 3\cdot \sqrt{17}\cdot\sqrt{17} \\\\
&= 3 \cdot 17 \\\\
&= 51
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The area of $\triangle ABC$ is $51$ units$^2$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Triangle A B C has point A at negative six, four, point B at six, seven, and point C at four, negative two. There is a slanted dashed line connecting from point C to a point D on side A B at a ninety degree angle at two, six.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the area, in square units, of $\triangle ABC$ plotted below.**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

$\text{Area of a triangle} = \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}$

Before we can apply the formula to find the area of $\triangle ABC$, we must find the base and height of the triangle using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

* The base is the length $BC$.
* The height is the length $AD$ (the base and the height always meet at a right angle).

###Step 1: Find the length $BC$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
BC^2&= {\redE{10}^2 +\redE{2}^2 }  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
BC&= \sqrt{\redE{10}^2 +\redE{2}^2 }  &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\sqrt{104} \\\\
&=\sqrt{4\cdot26} \\\\
&=\tealE{2\sqrt{26}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Find the length $AD$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
AD^2&= {\redE{10}^2 +\redE{2}^2 }  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AD&= \sqrt{\redE{10}^2 +\redE{2}^2 }  &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\sqrt{104} \\\\
&=\sqrt{4\cdot26} \\\\
&=\goldE{2\sqrt{26}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Find the area of the triangle

Using the base and height of $\triangle ABC$, we can find the area:

$\begin{align} \text{Area of a triangle} &= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot \text{base} \cdot \text{height}\\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot BC\cdot AD \\\\
&= \dfrac{1}{2}\cdot(\tealE{2\sqrt{26}})\cdot(\goldE{2\sqrt{26}}) \\\\
&= 2\cdot \sqrt{26}\cdot\sqrt{26} \\\\
&= 2 \cdot 26 \\\\
&= 52
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The area of $\triangle ABC$ is $52$ units$^2$.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Triangle A B C has point A at zero, seven, point B at seven, negative two, and point C at negative three, negative four. There is a slanted dashed line connecting from point A to a point D on side B C at a ninety degree angle at two, negative three.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the approximate perimeter of $\triangle ABC$ plotted below.** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a triangle is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Side $\overline{CA}$ is perfectly horizontal, so we can find its length using the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints.

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 3: Finding the perimeter

Side $\overline{CA}$ is perfectly horizontal, so we can use the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints to see that this side is $4$ units long.

Using the lengths of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$, we can find the perimeter of $\triangle{ABC}$:

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CA
\\\\
&= \tealE{\sqrt{136}}+\goldE{\sqrt{200}}+4
\\\\
&\approx 11.66+14.14+4
\\\\
&\approx 29.8
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The approximate perimeter of $\triangle ABC$ is $29.8$ units.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Triangle A B C has point A at two, seven, point B at negative four, negative three, and point C at six, seven.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the exact perimeter of pentagon $ABCDE$ plotted below.**  



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a pentagon is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Note that $AB=EA$ and $BC=DE$, so we only need to find the lengths of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, and $\overline{CD}$.

Side $\overline{CD}$ is perfectly horizontal, so we can find its length using the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints.

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 1: Finding the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} AB^2&= {\redE{6}^2 +\redE{7}^2 } &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{6}^2 +\redE{7}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\tealE{\sqrt{85}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Finding the length $BC$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
BC^2&= \redE{3}^2 +\redE{4}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
BC&= \sqrt{\redE{3}^2 +\redE{4}^2 }   &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\sqrt{25} \\\\
&=\goldE{5}
\end{align}$

###The answer

The exact perimeter of pentagon $ABCDE$ is $16+2\sqrt{85}$ units (approximately $34.4$ units).

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Pentagon A B C D E has point A at zero, seven, point B at six, zero, point C at three, negative four, point D at negative three, negative four, and point E negative six, zero. Sides B C and D E are congruent. Sides A B and A E are congruent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the exact perimeter of hexagon $ABCDEF$ plotted below.**  



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a hexagon is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Some sides are perfectly horizontal or vertical. We can find those lengths using just the $x$- or $y$-coordinates of the endpoints of those sides.

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{DE}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 1: Finding the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} AB^2&= {\redE{3}^2 +\redE{7}^2 } &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{3}^2 +\redE{7}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\tealE{\sqrt{58}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Finding the length $DE$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
DE^2&= \redE{5}^2 +\redE{2}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
DE&= \sqrt{\redE{5}^2 +\redE{2}^2 }   &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\goldE{\sqrt{29}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Finding the horizontal and vertical lengths

If a side is horizontal, we can use the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints to find its length.

If a side is vertical, we can use the $y$-coordinates of its endpoints to find its length.

* $\overline{BC}$ is $5$ units long
* $\overline{CD}$ is $6$ units long
* $\overline{EF}$ is $7$ units long
* $\overline{FA}$ is $5$ units long

###Step 4: Finding the perimeter

Using the lengths of all $6$ sides, we can find the perimeter of hexagon $ABCDEF$:

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CD+DE+EF+FA
\\\\
&= \tealE{\sqrt{58}}+5+6+\goldE{\sqrt{29}}+7+5
\\\\
&=23+\sqrt{29}+\sqrt{58}
\end{align}$

###The answer

The exact perimeter of hexagon $ABCDEF$ is $23+\sqrt{29}+\sqrt{58}$ units (approximately $36.0$ units).

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Hexagon A B C D E F has point A at negative six, two, point B at one, five, point C at six, five, point D at six, negative one, point E one, negative three, and point F at negative six, negative three.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the approximate perimeter of $\triangle ABC$ plotted below.** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a triangle is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, and $\overline{CA}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 4: Finding the perimeter

Using the lengths of all $3$ sides, we can find the perimeter of $\triangle ABC$:

$\begin{align} \text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CA \\\\
&=   \tealE{\sqrt{8}}+\goldE{\sqrt{74}}+\goldE{\sqrt{74}} \\\\
&\approx 2.83+8.60+8.60 \\\\
&\approx 20.0
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The approximate perimeter of $\triangle ABC$ is $20.0$ units.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Triangle A B C has point A at negative four, one, point B at negative two, three, and point C at three, negative four.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the approximate perimeter of pentagon $ABCDE$ plotted below.** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a pentagon is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Note that $BC=DE$, so we only need to find the lengths of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, $\overline{CD}$, and $\overline{EA}$.

Some sides are perfectly horizontal or vertical. We can find those lengths using just the $x$- or $y$-coordinates of the endpoints of those sides.

We can find the length of $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{CD}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 3: Finding the horizontal and vertical lengths

If a side is horizontal, we can use the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints to find its length.

If a side is vertical, we can use the $y$-coordinates of its endpoints to find its length.

* $\overline{AB}$ is $4$ units long
* $\overline{EA}$ is $4$ units long

###Step 4: Finding the perimeter

Using the lengths of all $5$ sides, we can find the perimeter of pentagon $ABCDE$:

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CD+DE+EA
\\\\
&= 4+ \tealE{\sqrt{89}}+\goldE{\sqrt{32}}+\tealE{\sqrt{89}}+4
\\\\
&\approx 4+9.43+5.66+9.43+4 \\\\
&\approx 32.5
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The approximate perimeter of pentagon $ABCDE$ is $32.5$ units.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Pentagon A B C D E has point A at negative six, five, point B at negative two, five, point C at six, zero, point D at two, negative four, and point E negative six, one. Sides B C and D E are congruent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the exact perimeter of hexagon $ABCDEF$ plotted below.**  



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a hexagon is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Note that $FA=BC$ and $CD=EF$, so we only need to find the lengths of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, $\overline{CD}$, and $\overline{DE}$.

Some sides are perfectly horizontal. We can find those lengths using just the $x$-coordinates of the endpoints of those sides.

We can find the length of sides $\overline{BC}$ and $\overline{CD}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 2: Finding the length $CD$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
CD^2&= \redE{6}^2 +\redE{7}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
CD&= \sqrt{\redE{6}^2 +\redE{7}^2 }   &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\goldE{\sqrt{85}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Finding the horizontal lengths

If a side is horizontal, we can use the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints to find its length.

* $\overline{AB}$ is $11$ units long
* $\overline{DE}$ is $5$ units long

###The answer

The exact perimeter of hexagon $ABCDEF$ is $16+2\sqrt{73}+2\sqrt{85}$ units (approximately $51.5$ units).

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Hexagon A B C D E F has point A at negative six, nine, point B at five, nine, point C at eight, one, point D at two, negative six, point E negative three, negative six, and point F at negative nine, one. Sides C D and E F are congruent. Sides A F and B C are congruent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the approximate perimeter of rectangle $ABCD$ plotted below.** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a rectangle is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Note that $AB=CD$ and $BC=DA$ since opposite sides in a rectangle have equal length. So we only need to find the lengths of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$.

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 3: Finding the perimeter

Using the lengths of all $4$ sides, we can find the perimeter of rectangle $ABCD$:

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CD+DA
\\\\
&= \tealE{\sqrt{125}}+\goldE{\sqrt{80}}+\tealE{\sqrt{125}}+\goldE{\sqrt{80}}
\\\\
&\approx 11.18+8.94+11.18+8.94 \\\\
&\approx 40.2
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The approximate perimeter of rectangle $ABCD$ is $40.2$ units.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Rectangle A B C D has point A at negative six, zero, point B at four, five, point C at eight, negative three, and point D at negative two, negative eight.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the approximate perimeter of rectangle $ABCD$ plotted below.** 



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a rectangle is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Note that $AB=CD$ and $BC=DA$ since opposite sides in a rectangle have equal length. So we only need to find the lengths of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$.

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 3: Finding the perimeter

Using the lengths of all $4$ sides, we can find the perimeter of rectangle $ABCD$:

$\begin{align}
\text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CD+DA
\\\\
&= \tealE{\sqrt{34}}+\goldE{\sqrt{136}}+\tealE{\sqrt{34}}+\goldE{\sqrt{136}}
\\\\
&\approx 5.83+11.66+5.83+11.66 \\\\
&\approx 35.0
\end{align}$

###The answer:

The approximate perimeter of rectangle $ABCD$ is $35.0$ units.

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Rectangle A B C D has point A at negative two, six, point B at three, three, point C at negative three, negative seven, and point D at negative eight, negative four.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the exact perimeter of quadrilateral $ABCD$ plotted below.**  



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a quadrilateral is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Side $\overline{CD}$ is perfectly horizontal, so we can find its length using the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints.

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, and $\overline{DA}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 1: Finding the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} AB^2&= {\redE{9}^2 +\redE{4}^2 } &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{9}^2 +\redE{4}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\tealE{\sqrt{97}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Finding the length $BC$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
BC^2&= \redE{3}^2 +\redE{4}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
BC&= \sqrt{\redE{3}^2 +\redE{4}^2 }   &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\sqrt{25} \\\\
&=\goldE{5}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Finding the length $DA$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
DA^2&= \redE{3}^2 +\redE{8}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
DA&= \sqrt{\redE{3}^2 +\redE{8}^2 }   &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\purpleD{\sqrt{73}}
\end{align}$

###Step 4: Finding the perimeter

Side $\overline{CD}$ is perfectly horizontal, so we can use the $x$-coordinates of its endpoints to see that this side is $3$ units long.

Using the lengths of all $4$ sides, we can find the perimeter of quadrilateral $ABCD$:

$\begin{align} \text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CD+DA \\\\
&=\tealE{\sqrt{97}}+\goldE{5}+3+\purpleD{\sqrt{73}} \\\\
&= 8+\sqrt{97}+\sqrt{73}
\end{align}$

###The answer

The exact perimeter of quadrilateral $ABCD$ is $8+\sqrt{97}+\sqrt{73}$ units (approximately $26.4$ units).

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at negative three, two, point B at six, negative two, point C at three, negative six, and point D at zero, negative six.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the exact perimeter of quadrilateral $ABCD$ plotted below.**  



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The strategy

The perimeter of a quadrilateral is the sum of the lengths of all the sides. 

Note that $BC=DA$, so we only need to find the lengths of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, and $\overline{CD}.$

We can find the length of $\overline{AB}$, $\overline{BC}$, and $\overline{CD}$ using the Pythagorean theorem (or the distance formula).

###Step 1: Finding the length $AB$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} AB^2&= {\redE{6}^2 +\redE{7}^2 } &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
AB&= \sqrt{\redE{6}^2 +\redE{7}^2 } &&\text{Square root both sides}\\\\
&=\sqrt{36+49} \\\\
&=\tealE{\sqrt{85}}
\end{align}$

###Step 2: Finding the length $BC$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
BC^2&= \redE{4}^2 +\redE{7}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
BC&= \sqrt{\redE{4}^2 +\redE{7}^2 }   &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\sqrt{16+49} \\\\
&=\goldE{\sqrt{65}}
\end{align}$

###Step 3: Finding the length $CD$



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} 
CD^2&= \redE{3}^2 +\redE{4}^2  &&\text{Pythagorean theorem} \\\\
CD&= \sqrt{\redE{3}^2 +\redE{4}^2 }   &&\text{Square root both sides} \\\\
&=\sqrt{25} \\\\
&=\purpleD{5}
\end{align}$

###Step 4: Finding the perimeter

Using the lengths of all $4$ sides, we can find the perimeter of quadrilateral $ABCD$:

$\begin{align} \text{Perimeter} &= AB+BC+CD+DA \\\\
&=\tealE{\sqrt{85}}+\goldE{\sqrt{65}}+\purpleD{5}+\goldE{\sqrt{65}} \\\\
&= 5+\sqrt{85}+2\sqrt{65}
\end{align}$

###The answer

The exact perimeter of quadrilateral $ABCD$ is $5+\sqrt{85}+2\sqrt{65}$ units (approximately $30.3$ units).

options.alt: A coordinate plane. Quadrilateral A B C D has point A at negative five, negative two, point B at one, five, point C at five, negative two, and point D at two, negative six. Sides A D and B C are congruent.
